title: Brexit and the roads not taken
gist: Reviewing three histories on the Brexit 10th anniversary, McGlynn argues Britain's EU exit was contingent rather than destined, that Britain's post-Brexit economic performance has tracked the EU's own decline rather than diverging from it, and that the UK and EU are 'locked in the same cycle of decline'.

themes.theme: Britain's EU entry and exit were both contingent

insights.claim: Brexit was not pre-ordained; the idea of Britain as a teleologically destined 'awkward partner' is a judgment of hindsight, with both 1973 entry and 2016 exit driven by contemporary factors.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Counters deterministic narratives; UK-EU realignment remains path-dependent on contemporary politics, not fate.
insights.locator: An important approach of Poggiolini's book

insights.claim: Staying out of the ECSC and EEC made perfect sense for Britain at the time given its sterling zone, global Commonwealth trade, nationalised industries and Korean War-era cheap imports.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Reframes the 'lost opportunity' of non-membership as a rational contemporary choice, not a blunder.
insights.quote: it made perfect sense at the time for Britain to stay out of the ECSC and the EEC

insights.claim: The EU's true motivation was always political integration cloaked in economic language, evidenced by the dropping of 'economic' from European Economic Community to European Community to European Union.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Frames the EU project as inherently political, informing how its future federalising moves should be read.
insights.quote: note the dropping of the economic adjective

insights.claim: EU enlargement diluted UK sovereignty and a large immigration increase from accession states like Poland and Romania caused working-class concern over wage undercutting, housing and public services, driving the exit.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Identifies sovereignty-dilution plus immigration as the operative Brexit drivers, relevant to any rejoin debate.
insights.locator: as the EU expanded, so UK sovereignty was further

insights.claim: Britain was tolerated as a difficult partner because it was consistently the second (occasionally third) largest net contributor to the EU budget after Germany.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Net fiscal contribution, not goodwill, explains the EU's patience with UK opt-outs.
insights.quote: Britain was always only second, occasionally third, after Germany as the largest net contributor

insights.claim: Thatcher's shift from Europhile to Eurosceptic was crystallised at the June 1985 Milan Council, where a Mitterrand-Kohl-Craxi triumvirate ambushed her by forcing a procedural vote to extend majority voting and launch a new treaty.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Shows how EU integrationists used procedural manoeuvres to override national vetoes, a recurring institutional dynamic.
insights.locator: A particular episode during the Milan Council

insights.claim: Thatcher was trapped by her own ambition: vetoing the Milan process would have meant forgoing her cherished Single Market, so she accepted the integrationist bait.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Illustrates how a desired economic prize can be used to extract political concessions.
insights.quote: That was the bait that ensnared her in the trap.

themes.theme: Post-Brexit reality and the rejoin trajectory

insights.claim: Despite economist Ross Clark voting Remain, his analysis finds Britain's post-Brexit performance not noticeably worse than the EU's, with both locked in the same cycle of decline.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Undercuts both Brexit-doom and Brexit-boom narratives; UK relative performance is a wash versus the EU.
insights.quote: Britain and the EU are both locked in the same cycle of decline.
insights.locator: But Britain's performance has not been noticeably worse

insights.claim: Post-Brexit failings have mainly been political, with Brexit serving as a convenient scapegoat for a lack of political leadership rather than being a cure-all or new source of ills.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Attributes UK underperformance to governance, not the EU exit itself, reframing the policy debate.
insights.quote: a diverting scapegoat for the lack of political leadership

insights.claim: The current UK government and potential coalition partners are advocating much closer EU ties with possible intent to rejoin in a different guise, with Trump and Putin's trade barriers and military actions smoothing the way.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Flags a re-convergence trajectory in UK-EU relations driven partly by external geopolitical pressure.
insights.quote: advocating for the restoration of much closer ties

insights.claim: Clark characterises the EU as a kritocracy with a democratic deficit, where rights are laid down by lofty legal institutions and human-rights court rulings cannot be challenged by anyone.
insights.speaker: Sean McGlynn
insights.so_what: Names a structural critique of EU governance distinct from the usual economic framing.
insights.quote: Consultation with the people is distinctly limited and constricted within strict boundaries.
insights.locator: Clark is at his most excoriating
